revision.comment: ページ [[高知県護国神社]] を [[高知縣護國神社]] へ移動

revision.text:
{{神社| 名称=高知県護国神社| 所在地=高知県高知市吸江213| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|32|40|N|133|34|15|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=護国の[[英霊]]| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 創建=[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）| 例祭=[[4月12日]]・[[11月12日]]}}
'''高知県護国神社'''（こうちけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[高知県]][[高知市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。国難に殉じた高知県関係の護国の[[英霊]]4万1千余柱を祀る。
==祭神== 明治維新志士四天王とされる武市半平太命・坂本龍馬命・中岡慎太郎命・吉村寅太郎命をはじめとし、東征の陣で没した土佐藩士１０５柱。
慶応4年に起きた堺事件で没した11名の土佐藩士。 安芸郡勤皇党の二十三士。 幕末以来日清・日露、大東亜戦争にあたって国家公共の為に殉じた高知県にゆかりのある英霊。
高知県出身並びに、縁故ある護国の英霊、４万１千余柱。
==歴史== [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[土佐藩]]主[[山内豊範]]が藩校[[致知館]]で、[[武市半平太]]・[[坂本龍馬]]・[[中岡慎太郎]]・[[吉村虎太郎]]をはじめ東征の陣歿藩士105柱を招魂し、その御霊を鎮祭するため、現在の五台山大島岬に大島岬の地に社域を定め、翌年の[[明治2年]]に建社の土地に因んで大島岬神社と称して社殿竣工とともに神霊を鎮座地に奉還したことに始まる。明治8年5月31日に[[招魂社]]と改称、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）に高知県護国神社に改称して特定護国神社となった。[[第二次大戦]]後は占領下における宗教法人令によって国の管理を離れ、社号も元の大島岬神社と称していたが、日本の主権回復後の[[昭和34年]]に元の社名に復した。[[昭和41年]]7月1日、[[神社本庁]]の別表神社に加列する。
[[Category:高知県の神社|こうちけんここくしんしや]] [[Category:護国神社|こうちけんここくしんしや]] [[Category:別表神社|こうちけんここくしんしや]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{神社| 名称=高知県護国神社| 所在地=高知県高知市吸江213| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|32|40|N|133|34|15|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=護国の[[英霊]]| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 創建=[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）| 例祭=[[4月2日]]・[[11月2日]]}}
'''高知県護国神社'''（こうちけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[高知県]][[高知市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。国難に殉じた高知県関係の護国の[[英霊]]4万1千余柱を祀る。 高知県護国神社奉賛会がある。
==祭神== 明治維新志士四天王とされる[[武市半平太]]命・[[坂本龍馬]]命・[[中岡慎太郎]]命・[[吉村寅太郎]]命をはじめとし、東征の陣で没した土佐藩士105柱。
[[堺事件]]（慶応4年）で没した11名の土佐藩士。
幕末以来日清・日露、大東亜戦争にあたって国家公共の為に殉じた高知県にゆかりのある英霊。
高知県出身並びに、縁故ある護国の英霊、４万１千余柱。
==歴史== [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[土佐藩]]主[[山内豊範]]が藩校[[致知館]]で、[[武市半平太]]・[[坂本龍馬]]・[[中岡慎太郎]]・[[吉村虎太郎]]をはじめ東征の陣歿藩士105柱を招魂し、その御霊を鎮祭するため、現在の五台山大島岬に大島岬の地に社域を定め、翌年の[[明治2年]]に建社の土地に因んで大島岬神社と称して社殿竣工とともに神霊を鎮座地に奉還したことに始まる。 明治8年5月31日に[[招魂社]]と改称、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）に高知県護国神社に改称して特定護国神社となった。 [[第二次大戦]]後は占領下における宗教法人令によって国の管理を離れ、社号も元の大島岬神社と称していたが、崇敬者多数の熱望により、[[昭和34年]]元の社名に復した。 [[昭和41年]]7月1日、[[神社本庁]]の別表神社に加列する。
==境内社== 開拓神社
==主な祭日== '''春季大祭(宵宮)''' 4月 1日 '''春季大祭''' 4月 2日 '''みたま祭''' 8月15日
'''秋季大祭(宵宮)''' 11月 1日 '''秋季大祭''' 11月 2日
'''命月祭''' 毎月10日・20日・27日
'''命日祭''' 毎朝午前7時

{{神社| 名称=高知県護国神社| 所在地=高知県高知市吸江213| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|32|40|N|133|34|15|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=護国の[[英霊]]| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 創建=[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）| 例祭=[[4月2日]]・[[11月2日]]}}
'''高知県護国神社'''（こうちけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[高知県]][[高知市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。国難に殉じた高知県関係の護国の[[英霊]]4万1千余柱を祀る。 高知県護国神社奉賛会がある。
== 祭神 == 明治維新志士四天王とされる[[武市瑞山|武市半平太]]命・[[坂本龍馬]]命・[[中岡慎太郎]]命・[[吉村寅太郎]]命をはじめとし、東征の陣で没した土佐藩士105柱。
[[堺事件]]（慶応4年）で没した11名の土佐藩士。
幕末以来日清・日露、大東亜戦争にあたって国家公共の為に殉じた高知県にゆかりのある英霊。
高知県出身並びに、縁故ある護国の英霊、4万1千余柱。
== 歴史 == [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[土佐藩]]主[[山内豊範]]が藩校[[高知致道館]]で、武市半平太・坂本龍馬・中岡慎太郎・吉村虎太郎をはじめ東征の陣歿藩士105柱を招魂し、その御霊を鎮祭するため、現在の五台山大島岬に大島岬の地に社域を定め、翌年の[[明治2年]]に建社の土地に因んで大島岬神社と称して社殿竣工とともに神霊を鎮座地に奉還したことに始まる。 明治8年5月31日に[[招魂社]]と改称、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）に高知県護国神社に改称して特定護国神社となった。 [[第二次大戦]]後は占領下における宗教法人令によって国の管理を離れ、社号も元の大島岬神社と称していたが、崇敬者多数の熱望により、[[1959年]]（昭和34年）元の社名に復した。 [[1966年]]（昭和41年）7月1日、[[神社本庁]]の別表神社に加列する。
== 境内社 == 開拓神社
== 主な祭日 == '''春季大祭（宵宮）''' 4月 1日 '''春季大祭''' 4月 2日 '''みたま祭''' 8月15日
'''秋季大祭（宵宮）''' 11月 1日 '''秋季大祭''' 11月 2日

'''命日祭''' 毎朝午前7時

== 歴史 == [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[土佐藩]]主[[山内豊範]]が藩校[[高知致道館]]で、[[迅衝隊]]などの東征に従軍し陣歿した藩士105柱を招魂し、その御霊を鎮祭するため、現在の五台山大島岬に大島岬の地に社域を定め、翌年の[[明治2年]]に建社の土地に因んで大島岬神社と称して社殿竣工とともに神霊を鎮座地に奉還したことに始まる。 明治8年5月31日に[[招魂社]]と改称、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）に高知県護国神社に改称して特定護国神社となった。 [[第二次大戦]]後は占領下における宗教法人令によって国の管理を離れ、社号も元の大島岬神社と称していたが、崇敬者多数の熱望により、[[1959年]]（昭和34年）元の社名に復した。 [[1966年]]（昭和41年）7月1日、[[神社本庁]]の別表神社に加列する。
== 境内社 == *開拓神社

{{神社| 名称=高知県護国神社| 所在地=高知県高知市吸江213| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|32|40|N|133|34|15|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=護国の[[英霊]]| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 創建=[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）| 例祭=[[4月2日]]・[[11月2日]]}}
'''高知県護国神社'''（こうちけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[高知県]][[高知市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。国難に殉じた高知県関係の護国の[[英霊]]4万1千余柱を祀る。 高知県護国神社奉賛会がある。
== 祭神 == [[迅衝隊]]など、[[戊辰戦争]]の際に東征の陣で没した土佐藩士105柱。 明治維新志士四天王とされる[[武市瑞山|武市半平太]]命・[[坂本龍馬]]命・[[中岡慎太郎]]命・[[吉村虎太郎|吉村寅太郎]]命。
[[堺事件]]（慶応4年）で没した11名の土佐藩士。
幕末以来日清・日露、大東亜戦争にあたって国家公共の為に殉じた高知県にゆかりのある英霊。
高知県出身並びに、縁故ある護国の英霊、4万1千余柱。

== 歴史 == [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[土佐藩]]主[[山内豊範]]が藩校[[高知致道館]]で、[[迅衝隊]]などの東征に従軍し陣歿した藩士105柱を招魂し、その御霊を鎮祭するため、現在の五台山大島岬に大島岬の地に社域を定め、翌年の[[明治2年]]に建社の土地に因んで大島岬神社と称して社殿竣工とともに神霊を鎮座地に奉還したことに始まる。 明治8年5月31日に[[招魂社]]と改称、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）に高知県護国神社に改称して特定護国神社となった。 [[第二次大戦]]後は占領下における宗教法人令によって国の管理を離れ、社号も元の大島岬神社と称していたが、崇敬者多数の熱望により、[[1959年]]（昭和34年）元の社名に復した。 [[1966年]]（昭和41年）7月1日、[[神社本庁]]の別表神社に加列する。

[[堺事件]]（慶応4年）で没した11名の土佐藩士。
幕末以来日清・日露、大東亜戦争にあたって国家公共の為に殉じた高知県にゆかりのある戦没者。
高知県出身並びに、縁故ある戦没者4万1千余柱。
== 歴史 == [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[土佐藩]]主[[山内豊範]]が藩校[[高知致道館]]で、[[迅衝隊]]などの東征に従軍し陣歿した藩士105柱を招魂し、その御霊を鎮祭するため、現在の五台山大島岬に大島岬の地に社域を定め、翌年の[[明治2年]]に建社の土地に因んで大島岬神社と称して社殿竣工とともに神霊を鎮座地に奉還したことに始まる。 明治8年5月31日に[[招魂社]]と改称、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）に高知県護国神社に改称して特定護国神社となった。 [[第二次大戦]]後は占領下における宗教法人令によって国の管理を離れ、社号も元の大島岬神社と称していたが、崇敬者多数の熱望により、[[1959年]]（昭和34年）元の社名に復した。 [[1966年]]（昭和41年）7月1日、[[神社本庁]]の別表神社に加列する。
== 境内社 == *開拓神社
== 主な祭日 == * '''春季大祭（宵宮）''' 4月1日 - '''春季大祭''' 4月2日 - '''みたま祭''' 8月15日 * '''秋季大祭（宵宮）''' 11月1日 - '''秋季大祭''' 11月2日 * '''命月祭''' 毎月10日・20日・27日 * '''命日祭''' 毎朝午前7時
== 関連項目 == * [[戊辰戦争]] * [[迅衝隊]] * [[武市瑞山]] * [[坂本龍馬]] * [[中岡慎太郎]] * [[吉村虎太郎]]

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kotikengokokujinja.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:高知縣護國神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Kōchi Gokoku Jinja Yokaren Monument |url=https://www.kamikazeimages.net/monuments/kochi-yokaren/index.htm |access-date=2023-10-22 |website=www.kamikazeimages.net}} It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017}} {{Shrine |Name=Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine |Image=[[File:Kotikengokokujinja.JPG|250px]] |Location=Kochi Prefecture, Kochi City, Suiego 213 |ISO=JP-39 |Latitude degrees=33|Latitude minutes=32|Latitude seconds=40.5|N/S=N |Longitude degrees=133|Longitude minutes=34|Longitude seconds=16|E/W=E |Map country code=JP |Deity=National Martyrs |Rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Special Shrine |Founded=Meiji 2 (1869) |Annual festival=April 2, November 2 |Map=Japan Kochi city |Label position=left }} '''Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (こうちけんごこくじんじゃ{{ill|Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知県護国神社}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kochi City|ja|高知市}}, {{ill|Kochi Prefecture|ja|高知県}}. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Special Shrines]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Wikipfedia drafts]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

'''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Special Shrines]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kotikengokokujinja.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:高知縣護國神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Kōchi Gokoku Jinja Yokaren Monument |url=https://www.kamikazeimages.net/monuments/kochi-yokaren/index.htm |access-date=2023-10-22 |website=www.kamikazeimages.net}} It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017}} {{Shrine |Name=Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine |Image=[[File:Kotikengokokujinja.JPG|250px]] |Location=Kochi Prefecture, Kochi City, Suiego 213 |ISO=JP-39 |Latitude degrees=33|Latitude minutes=32|Latitude seconds=40.5|N/S=N |Longitude degrees=133|Longitude minutes=34|Longitude seconds=16|E/W=E |Map country code=JP |Deity=National Martyrs |Rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Special Shrine |Founded=Meiji 2 (1869) |Annual festival=April 2, November 2 |Map=Japan Kochi city |Label position=left }} '''Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (こうちけんごこくじんじゃ{{ill|Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知県護国神社}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kochi City|ja|高知市}}, {{ill|Kochi Prefecture|ja|高知県}}. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017}} {{Shrine |Name=Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine |Image=[[File:Kotikengokokujinja.JPG|250px]] |Location=Kochi Prefecture, Kochi City, Suiego 213 |ISO=JP-39 |Latitude degrees=33|Latitude minutes=32|Latitude seconds=40.5|N/S=N |Longitude degrees=133|Longitude minutes=34|Longitude seconds=16|E/W=E |Map country code=JP |Deity=National Martyrs |Rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Special Shrine |Founded=Meiji 2 (1869) |Annual festival=April 2, November 2 |Map=Japan Kochi city |Label position=left }} '''Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (こうちけんごこくじんじゃ{{ill|Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知県護国神社}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kochi City|ja|高知市}}, {{ill|Kochi Prefecture|ja|高知県}}. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:08 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称=高知県護国神社 |画像=[[File:Kotikengokokujinja.JPG|250px]]
石標と海軍之塔 |所在地=[[高知県]][[高知市]]吸江213 |緯度度 = 33 | 緯度分 = 32 | 緯度秒 = 40.5 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 133 |経度分 = 34 | 経度秒 = 16 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |地図国コード = JP |祭神=国事殉難者 |社格=内務大臣指定[[護国神社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建=[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]） |例祭=[[4月2日]]・[[11月2日]] |地図=Japan Kochi city |ラベル位置=left }} '''高知県護国神社'''（こうちけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[高知県]][[高知市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。国難に殉じた高知県関係の戦没者4万1千余柱を祀る。 高知県護国神社奉賛会がある。 == 祭神 == [[迅衝隊]]など、[[戊辰戦争]]の際に東征の陣で没した土佐藩士105柱。 明治維新志士四天王とされる[[武市瑞山|武市半平太]]命・[[坂本龍馬]]命・[[中岡慎太郎]]命・[[吉村虎太郎|吉村寅太郎]]命。 [[堺事件]]（慶応4年）で没した11名の土佐藩士。 幕末以来日清・日露、太平洋戦争（大東亜戦争）にあたって国家公共の為に殉じた高知県にゆかりのある戦没者。 高知県出身並びに、縁故ある戦没者4万1千余柱。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）、[[土佐藩]]主[[山内豊範]]が藩校[[高知致道館]]で、[[迅衝隊]]などの東征に従軍し陣歿した藩士105柱を招魂し、その御霊を鎮祭するため、現在の五台山大島岬に大島岬の地に社域を定め、翌年の[[明治2年]]に建社の土地に因んで大島岬神社と称して社殿竣工とともに神霊を鎮座地に奉還したことに始まる。 明治8年5月31日に[[招魂社]]と改称、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）に高知県護国神社に改称して特定護国神社となった。 [[第二次世界大戦|第二次大戦]]後は占領下における宗教法人令によって国の管理を離れ、社号も元の大島岬神社と称していたが、崇敬者多数の熱望により、[[1959年]]（昭和34年）元の社名に復した。 [[1966年]]（昭和41年）7月1日、[[神社本庁]]の別表神社に加列する。 == 境内社 == *開拓神社 == 主な祭日 == * '''春季大祭（宵宮）''' 4月1日 - '''春季大祭''' 4月2日 - '''みたま祭''' 8月15日　 * '''秋季大祭（宵宮）''' 11月1日 - '''秋季大祭''' 11月2日 * '''命月祭''' 毎月10日・20日・27日 * '''命日祭''' 毎朝午前7時 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[戊辰戦争]] * [[迅衝隊]] * [[武市瑞山]] * [[坂本龍馬]] * [[中岡慎太郎]] * [[吉村虎太郎]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:高知県の神社]] [[Category:高知市の建築物]] [[Category:高知市の歴史]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:迅衝隊|*ここくしんしや]]

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:Kochi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Kochi Gokoku Shrine]]}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:高知縣護國神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} (高知縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Kōchi Gokoku Jinja Yokaren Monument |url=https://www.kamikazeimages.net/monuments/kochi-yokaren/index.htm |access-date=2023-10-22 |website=www.kamikazeimages.net}} It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine[[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] {{draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kotikengokokujinja.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:高知縣護國神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} (高知縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Kōchi Gokoku Jinja Yokaren Monument |url=https://www.kamikazeimages.net/monuments/kochi-yokaren/index.htm |access-date=2023-10-22 |website=www.kamikazeimages.net}} It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * Gokoku Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kochi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kochi There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:高知縣護國神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} (高知縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Kōchi Gokoku Jinja Yokaren Monument |url=https://www.kamikazeimages.net/monuments/kochi-yokaren/index.htm |access-date=2023-10-22 |website=www.kamikazeimages.net}} It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == Related Articles == * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura {{draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kotikengokokujinja.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:高知縣護國神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} (高知縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Kōchi Gokoku Jinja Yokaren Monument |url=https://www.kamikazeimages.net/monuments/kochi-yokaren/index.htm |access-date=2023-10-22 |website=www.kamikazeimages.net}} It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} == References == {{reflist}}{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kochi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * revision.text:
{{draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kotikengokokujinja.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:高知縣護國神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kochi Gokoku Shrine|ja|高知縣護國神社}} (高知縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Kōchi Gokoku Jinja Yokaren Monument |url=https://www.kamikazeimages.net/monuments/kochi-yokaren/index.htm |access-date=2023-10-22 |website=www.kamikazeimages.net}} It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} It is on a hill. It enshrines over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture who died in national crises. There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}} == References == {{reflist}}{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうちけんごこくじんじゃ}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kochi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kochi City]] [[Category:History of Kochi City]] [[Category:Jinshōtai]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Kōchi, Kōchi]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kōchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kochi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine {{DEFAULTSORT:Kochi Gokoku Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kochi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Kochi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Kochi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:ファイル]] There is a Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Support Association. == Deities == The shrine enshrines: * 105 Tosa domain samurai who died in the Boshin War during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}}. * Four Great Loyalists of the Meiji Restoration: {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} (Takechi Hanpeita), {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}}, {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}}, and {{ill|Yoshimura Torataro|ja|吉村虎太郎}}. * 11 Tosa domain samurai who died in the {{ill|Sakai Incident|ja|堺事件}} (Keio 4). * Tosa domain samurai and others who died in the Boshin War, Sino-Japanese War, Russo-Japanese War, and the Pacific War (Great East Asia War). * Over 41,000 war dead from Kochi Prefecture and those with connections to the prefecture. == History == In Meiji 1 (1868), the Tosa domain lord {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|ja|山内豊範}} held a requiem for 105 Tosa domain samurai who died during the Eastern Expedition, including members of the Jinshōtai, at the domain school {{ill|Kochi Chidokan|ja|高知致道館}}. To enshrine their spirits, a shrine was established at Oshima Cape on Godaisan Mountain, named Oshima Cape Shrine, and the shrine buildings were completed the following year in Meiji 2 (1869). On May 31, Meiji 8 (1875), it was renamed {{ill|Shokonsha|ja|招魂社}}. In Showa 14 (1939), it was renamed Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine and designated a specific Gokoku Shrine. After World War II, it was no longer managed by the state due to the Religious Corporations Ordinance under the occupation, and its name reverted to Oshima Cape Shrine. In 1959 (Showa 34), due to the strong wishes of many worshippers, its name was restored to Kochi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. On July 1, 1966 (Showa 41), it was listed as a special shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kaitaku Shrine == Major Festivals == * '''Spring Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' April 1 - '''Spring Grand Festival''' April 2 - '''Mitama Festival''' August 15 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (Evening Festival)''' November 1 - '''Autumn Grand Festival''' November 2 * '''Reisai''' every 10th, 20th, and 27th of the month * '''Memorial Day Festival''' every morning at 7 AM == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] * {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}} * {{ill|Jinshōtai|ja|迅衝隊}} * {{ill|Takechi Zuizan|ja|武市瑞山}} * {{ill|Sakamoto Ryoma|ja|坂本龍馬}} * {{ill|Nakaoka Shintaro|ja|中岡慎太郎}} * {{ill|Yoshimura Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K.
